question_text: During a heating experiment, 100 g of a solid substance is heated steadily. The heating curve shows: 0 to 5 minutes (temperature rises from 20°C to 80°C), 5 to 12 minutes (temperature stays at 80°C), 12 to 18 minutes (temperature rises from 80°C to 100°C). Calculate the time taken for the latent heat of fusion and explain what is happening to the particles during this period.
correct_answer: The latent heat of fusion occurs between 5 and 12 minutes, which is 7 minutes. During this time, the substance is melting: particles in the solid are breaking free from their fixed positions and becoming a liquid, but they are not moving faster (temperature is constant), so all energy goes into breaking the bonds holding the solid structure together.
explanation: The flat section on the heating curve (5 to 12 minutes) shows melting because temperature is constant while energy is being added. This 7-minute period is when latent heat is absorbed to change the state from solid to liquid without increasing particle speed.
